python.explanation:
This pure Python implementation manually builds the dropout mask by sampling a uniform random value per element and comparing it to the dropout probability p. Elements where the random value falls below p are zeroed out; the rest are kept and multiplied by the inverted keep-probability scale factor 1/(1-p). This scaling (called inverted dropout) is applied during the forward pass so that inference requires no adjustment — outputs simply pass through unchanged when training=False.

The mask is stored as self.mask after each forward call so the backward pass can reuse exactly the same binary pattern. Gradients flow only through the neurons that were active during the forward pass, scaled by the same factor. This ensures the expected gradient magnitude is preserved, which is critical for stable training.

Using Python's built-in random module keeps this solution dependency-free but is significantly slower than vectorized alternatives for large arrays.
key_learnings: Inverted dropout scales kept neurons by 1/(1-p) during training so inference is a no-op — the alternative (scaling at test time) is less common in practice.
The mask must be identical in forward and backward; storing self.mask is the canonical pattern for stateful layers.
Dropout rate p is the probability of zeroing, so keep-probability is (1-p) and the scale factor is its reciprocal.
When p=0 or training=False, the layer is an identity function — handle these short-circuit cases explicitly.
Dropout is applied independently per element and per forward call; regenerating the mask each step is essential for regularization to work.

numpy.explanation:
The NumPy implementation uses np.random.rand to generate a uniform [0,1) sample array with the same shape as the input in a single vectorized call. Comparing elementwise against p yields a boolean mask; casting to the input dtype and dividing by (1-p) produces the final scaled float mask stored in self.mask.

Multiplying x by self.mask in one NumPy operation is orders of magnitude faster than the pure Python loop because NumPy delegates the work to optimized C routines. The backward pass is equally simple — gradient arrays have the same shape as inputs, so applying the stored mask is a single element-wise multiply.

This is the standard reference implementation you would find in educational deep learning frameworks like micrograd or tinygrad. It illustrates the core idea clearly without framework overhead while being fully production-correct for CPU workloads.
key_learnings: np.random.rand(*x.shape) generates the full mask in one call — avoid Python loops over array elements for performance-critical paths.
Casting the boolean mask to x.dtype before dividing preserves numerical precision and avoids implicit type promotion issues.
The backward pass is structurally identical to the forward pass because dropout is element-wise and its 'derivative' is just the same binary mask.
Storing the mask as a float (not bool) means you only need one multiply in both passes — no separate scale application.
During inference (training=False), returning x unchanged (not a copy) is correct and avoids an unnecessary allocation.

tensorflow.explanation:
Two implementations are shown. The first (DropoutLayer) wraps NumPy arrays in TF tensors, applies the dropout using tf.random.uniform, and returns numpy arrays — matching the problem's interface while leveraging TF's random ops. The mask is stored as a numpy array for the manual backward pass.

The second (DropoutLayerTF) is the idiomatic Keras subclass pattern. By overriding call() and accepting a training argument, it integrates seamlessly with Keras model.fit() and the training=True/False lifecycle. The GradientTape demo shows that TF automatically differentiates through the mask multiplication — you don't need to implement backward manually in the Keras world because autograd traces through the computation graph.

In both cases the inverted dropout scaling is identical: divide the binary mask by (1-p) once during forward so the backward gradient flows through the same scaled mask automatically.
key_learnings: tf.keras.layers.Layer subclasses use the training argument in call() — this is the standard hook for train/eval mode switching in Keras.
GradientTape automates the backward pass: dropout's gradient is just the mask applied to upstream gradients, which TF derives automatically.
tf.random.uniform produces values in [0,1); comparing >= p gives the keep mask with the correct Bernoulli(1-p) distribution.
Storing the mask inside the layer object is necessary for manual backward but unnecessary under GradientTape — understanding both patterns is interview-relevant.
Keras's built-in tf.keras.layers.Dropout handles seeding, distribution strategies, and graph mode — prefer it in production over manual implementations.

pytorch.explanation:
Three layers of abstraction are shown. The first (DropoutLayer) directly mirrors the problem interface using torch.rand_like for vectorized mask generation, storing the mask as a numpy array for the manual backward pass. This is the simplest correct solution.

The second approach implements a custom torch.autograd.Function. This is the canonical PyTorch pattern for operations that need explicit forward/backward control. ctx.save_for_backward stores the mask in the autograd graph, and the backward method returns masked gradients. Returning None for the p and training arguments tells autograd those inputs don't need gradients.

The nn.Module wrapper (DropoutLayerTorch) integrates with PyTorch's training lifecycle: self.training is automatically set by model.train() and model.eval(), so no explicit training flag is needed at call sites. This is how torch.nn.Dropout is actually implemented internally.
key_learnings: torch.autograd.Function is the escape hatch for custom gradients — implement forward/backward as @staticmethod and use ctx.save_for_backward for tensors needed in the backward pass.
Return None from backward for non-Tensor or non-differentiable inputs (like the bool training flag and float p) — the count of return values must match the number of forward inputs.
nn.Module.training is automatically managed by .train()/.eval() — prefer self.training over passing a manual flag when building production layers.
torch.rand_like(x) generates a tensor with the same shape, dtype, and device as x — always prefer this over torch.rand(*x.shape) to avoid device/dtype mismatches.
PyTorch's built-in nn.Dropout uses Bernoulli sampling under the hood and is fused with CUDA kernels for GPU efficiency — understanding the manual version reveals exactly what it does.
